firmware.name: RepRapFirmware
firmware.description: Advanced open-source firmware by Duet3D for CNC, 3D printing, and laser applications. All configuration via G-code (config.g). Supports runtime mode switching between FDM (M451), Laser (M452), and CNC (M453).

codes.name: Rapid Move
codes.description: Rapid linear move. In RepRapFirmware, G0 and G1 are IDENTICAL — the feed rate is always controlled by F. There is no separate rapid rate. This is a critical difference from grbl and LinuxCNC where G0 always moves at maximum traverse rate.

parameters.name: H
parameters.description: Motor stall detection / special moves: 0=no stall detection, 1=stall detect and stop, 2=stall detect and log. Replaced S parameter in RRF 3.x for homing moves.

examples.description: Homing move with stall detection enabled

crossReferences.note: WARNING: In grbl, G0 always moves at maximum rate regardless of F. In RRF, G0 and G1 are functionally identical — both use the F parameter. This is one of the most important behavioral differences between firmwares.
WARNING: LinuxCNC G0 ignores F and uses maximum traverse rate. RRF G0 respects F and is identical to G1.

modeNotes.description: G0 moves do not fire the laser regardless of any S parameter. Use G1 with S for laser cutting moves.

codes.notes: Key difference from NIST standard: G0 and G1 are functionally the same in RRF. The H parameter for stall detection / homing moves is unique to RRF. The S parameter was repurposed for laser power in RRF 2.02+ — old S homing behavior moved to H parameter in RRF 3.x.

codes.name: Linear Move
codes.description: Controlled linear move. Functionally identical to G0 in RepRapFirmware. In laser mode (M452), the S parameter on G1 directly controls laser power.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: Laser power (0.0-1.0) in laser mode (M452). Since RRF 2.02+, S was repurposed from its original homing function.

parameters.name: H
parameters.description: Motor stall detection / homing moves (replaced old S parameter in RRF 3.x): 0=no stall detection, 1=stall detect and stop, 2=stall detect and log

crossReferences.note: WARNING: Laser power is fundamentally different. In grbl, laser power is set with S on M3/M4 (integer 0-$30 max value). In RRF laser mode, S on G1 directly sets laser power as a fraction (0.0-1.0).
LinuxCNC uses S on M3/M4 for spindle/laser speed, not on G1. RRF's S-on-G1 for laser power is unique.

modeNotes.description: S parameter controls laser power as a fraction (0.0 to 1.0). Power is applied during the move and automatically turns off when motion stops. This is fundamentally different from grbl where laser power is set via M3/M4 S parameter.
S parameter has no special effect. Standard printing move.
S parameter has no special effect. Spindle speed is controlled via M3/M4.

codes.notes: The S parameter on G1 was repurposed in RRF 2.02+. In laser mode, S controls power directly on the move command. The old S parameter behavior (homing moves) was moved to the H parameter in RRF 3.x. This S-parameter repurposing is a significant behavioral difference from all other firmwares.

codes.name: Clockwise Arc
codes.description: Clockwise circular or helical interpolation in the selected plane (G17/G18/G19).

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: Arc center offset in X (incremental from current position)

parameters.name: J
parameters.description: Arc center offset in Y (incremental from current position)

parameters.name: K
parameters.description: Arc center offset in Z (for helical arcs in XZ/YZ planes)

parameters.name: R
parameters.description: Arc radius (alternative to IJK). Positive = minor arc, negative = major arc.

examples.description: CW arc to X10 Y10 with center offset I5 J0

crossReferences.note: Similar behavior. grbl only supports incremental IJK (G91.1). RRF supports both incremental (default) and absolute IJK modes.
Similar behavior. Both support full arc specification with IJK or R.

modeNotes.description: S parameter on arc commands controls laser power during the arc move.

codes.notes: Arcs are supported in all three planes (G17 XY, G18 XZ, G19 YZ). Full circles can be made by specifying only IJK with no endpoint change.

codes.name: Counter-Clockwise Arc
codes.description: Counter-clockwise circular or helical interpolation. Same as G2 but in the opposite direction.

examples.description: CCW arc to X10 Y10 with center offset I5 J0

crossReferences.note: Similar behavior.

modeNotes.description: S parameter controls laser power during the arc.

codes.notes: Same as G2 but counter-clockwise.

codes.name: Dwell
codes.description: Pause the machine for a specified time. Accepts time in milliseconds (P) or seconds (S).

crossReferences.note: grbl uses P for seconds (decimal allowed). RRF uses P for milliseconds and S for seconds — be careful with the P parameter unit difference.
LinuxCNC uses P for seconds. RRF uses P for milliseconds — a common source of errors when porting G-code.

codes.notes: WARNING: P parameter is in milliseconds in RRF, but in seconds in grbl and LinuxCNC. This is a frequent source of errors when converting G-code between firmwares. Use S for seconds to avoid confusion.

codes.name: Retract / Set Tool Offset / Set Workspace Offset
codes.description: Multi-purpose command. Without parameters: firmware retract. With L and P: set tool offsets or workspace coordinate offsets.

parameters.name: L
parameters.description: Offset type: L1 or L10 = set tool offsets, L2 = set workspace offset, L20 = set workspace offset to make current position the given value

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Tool number (for L1/L10) or workspace number 1-9 (for L2/L20, corresponding to G54-G59.3)

examples.code: G10 L2 P1 X10 Y10 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 work offset to X10 Y10 Z0

examples.code: G10 L20 P1 X0 Y0 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 so current position becomes X0 Y0 Z0

crossReferences.note: grbl supports G10 L2 and G10 L20 for workspace offsets only. RRF also supports tool offsets (L1/L10) and firmware retract (no parameters).
LinuxCNC supports G10 L1/L2/L10/L20 for tool and work offsets. Similar to RRF but RRF adds firmware retract with no parameters.

codes.notes: This is a multi-purpose command. With no parameters it performs firmware retract. With L2/L20 it sets workspace offsets (critical for CNC). With L1/L10 it sets tool offsets.

codes.name: Unretract
codes.description: Reverses a firmware retract performed by G10 (no parameters). Pushes filament back and optionally raises Z.
codes.notes: Only relevant in FDM mode. Pairs with G10 firmware retract. Retract/unretract distances are configured with M207.

codes.name: Select XY Plane
codes.description: Select XY plane for arc commands (G2/G3). This is the default plane.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: RRF 3.x added plane selection. Required for arcs in non-default planes.

codes.name: Select XZ Plane
codes.description: Select XZ plane for arc commands (G2/G3). Used for arcs on vertical surfaces.

examples.description: Select XZ plane for vertical arcs

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Added in RRF 3.x.

codes.name: Select YZ Plane
codes.description: Select YZ plane for arc commands (G2/G3).

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Added in RRF 3.x.

codes.name: Inch Units
codes.description: Set units to inches for subsequent G-code commands.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: RRF defaults to millimeters (G21). Inch mode affects F feed rate values as well.

codes.name: Millimeter Units
codes.description: Set units to millimeters for subsequent G-code commands. This is the default.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Default unit mode in RRF.

codes.name: Home Axes
codes.description: Homes the specified axes by running homing macro files (homeall.g, homex.g, homey.g, homez.g). The homing sequence is fully programmable via these macro files in the /sys directory. This is NOT a go-to-stored-position command.

crossReferences.note: WARNING: RRF G28 = homing (like grbl's $H). grbl does not use G28 for homing. grbl G28 goes to a stored predefined position.
WARNING: CONFLICT. LinuxCNC G28 moves to stored position (set by G28.1). RRF G28 performs homing. Completely different behavior!

codes.notes: WARNING: G28 meaning differs significantly between firmwares. In RRF, G28 = homing via macro files. In grbl and LinuxCNC, G28 = go to stored position. RRF homing is defined by macro files in /sys, making it extremely flexible. In RRF 3.x, homing moves inside macros use H1 parameter (was S1 in RRF 2.x).

codes.name: Mesh Bed Probing
codes.description: Performs mesh bed probing / compensation. Probes the bed at a grid of points to create a height map for compensation.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: 0=probe and save height map, 1=load saved height map, 2=clear height map

examples.description: Probe the bed and generate height map
Load a previously saved height map

crossReferences.note: grbl has no mesh probing. grbl uses G38.2-G38.5 for single-point probing only.

codes.notes: The probe grid is defined by M557. Typically used for 3D printing bed compensation but can also be useful for CNC surfacing operations.

codes.name: Single Probe
codes.description: Probe the bed at a single point. Used for Z height setting and as part of bed leveling.

parameters.name: X
parameters.description: X position to probe (optional, defaults to current)

parameters.name: Y
parameters.description: Y position to probe (optional, defaults to current)

parameters.name: Z
parameters.description: Z value to set when probe triggers (sets Z height)

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Probe point number for bed leveling (used with G32)

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: -1 = no action after probe (just report), other = set Z offset

examples.description: Probe at current position, set Z=0 at trigger point
Probe and report height without changing Z

crossReferences.note: grbl uses G38.2 for probing toward workpiece. RRF G30 is specific to Z-axis bed probing.

codes.notes: Commonly used to set Z zero for CNC operations. The probe type and trigger parameters are configured with G31.

codes.name: Set Probe Parameters
codes.description: Configure Z probe type, trigger height, trigger value, and probe offsets from the tool.

examples.description: Set probe trigger at 500, offset Z=2.5mm
Probe 25mm left of nozzle, triggers at 1.8mm

codes.notes: Must be configured before using G29 or G30. Probe offsets are critical for accurate bed probing.

codes.name: Bed Leveling
codes.description: Run the bed leveling macro (bed.g). Used for multi-point bed leveling with tilt compensation or auto-calibration on delta printers.
codes.notes: Runs the bed.g macro file which typically contains multiple G30 probe points. The firmware then calculates and applies leveling compensation.

codes.name: Straight Probe - Toward, Error on No Contact
codes.description: Probe toward workpiece. Stops on contact. Error if probe does not trigger before target position.

examples.description: Probe downward toward workpiece, error if no contact

crossReferences.note: Same behavior per NIST standard.
Same behavior per NIST standard.

codes.notes: Added in RRF 3.x. Standard NIST probing cycle. Useful for CNC tool setting and workpiece probing.

codes.name: Straight Probe - Toward, No Error
codes.description: Probe toward workpiece. Stops on contact. No error if probe does not trigger.

examples.description: Probe downward, no error if nothing contacted

crossReferences.note: Same behavior per NIST standard.

codes.notes: Added in RRF 3.x. Useful when you need to check if a surface exists without halting on failure.

codes.name: Straight Probe - Away, Error on No Release
codes.description: Probe away from workpiece. Stops when probe releases. Error if probe does not release before target position.

examples.description: Probe upward away from surface, error if no release

crossReferences.note: Same behavior per NIST standard.

codes.notes: Added in RRF 3.x. Used to find the exact release point after an initial probe contact.

codes.name: Straight Probe - Away, No Error
codes.description: Probe away from workpiece. Stops when probe releases. No error if probe does not release.

examples.description: Probe upward away from surface, no error if no release

crossReferences.note: Same behavior per NIST standard.

codes.notes: Added in RRF 3.x.

codes.name: Machine Coordinate System
codes.description: Move in machine (absolute) coordinates, ignoring any work coordinate offset. Non-modal — only applies to the line it appears on.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. Non-modal, one-line only.
Same behavior per NIST standard.

codes.notes: Non-modal: only affects the line on which it appears. Must be combined with G0 or G1 on the same line.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 1
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 1. This is the default work coordinate system.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. grbl supports G54-G59 (6 systems). RRF supports G54-G59.3 (9 systems).
Same behavior. LinuxCNC also supports G54-G59.3.

codes.notes: RRF supports 9 work coordinate systems: G54, G55, G56, G57, G58, G59, G59.1, G59.2, G59.3. Set offsets with G10 L2 or G10 L20.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 2
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 2.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.

codes.notes: Set offsets with G10 L2 P2 or G10 L20 P2.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 3
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 3.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.

codes.notes: Set offsets with G10 L2 P3 or G10 L20 P3.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 4
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 4.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.

codes.notes: Set offsets with G10 L2 P4 or G10 L20 P4.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 5
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 5.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.

codes.notes: Set offsets with G10 L2 P5 or G10 L20 P5.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 6
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 6.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.

codes.notes: Set offsets with G10 L2 P6 or G10 L20 P6.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 7
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 7. Extended coordinate system not available in grbl.

crossReferences.note: grbl only supports G54-G59 (6 systems). G59.1-G59.3 are NOT available in grbl.
Same behavior. LinuxCNC supports extended coordinate systems.

codes.notes: Extended WCS. Set offsets with G10 L2 P7 or G10 L20 P7.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 8
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 8.

crossReferences.note: Not available in grbl.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Extended WCS. Set offsets with G10 L2 P8 or G10 L20 P8.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 9
codes.description: Select work coordinate system 9.

crossReferences.note: Not available in grbl.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Extended WCS. Set offsets with G10 L2 P9 or G10 L20 P9.

codes.name: Save Position
codes.description: Save the current position to a numbered slot for later recall with G1 R parameter.

crossReferences.note: LinuxCNC uses G28.1 to save position for G28. RRF uses numbered slots with G60.

codes.notes: Supports multiple save slots. Saved positions can be recalled using G1 Rn (where n = slot number). Useful for tool change macros and return-to-position sequences.

codes.name: Coordinate Rotation
codes.description: Rotate the coordinate system around a specified center point by a given angle. UNIQUE to RRF among hobby-level CNC firmwares.

examples.description: Rotate coordinate system 45 degrees CCW around origin

crossReferences.note: UNIQUE to RRF: grbl does NOT support coordinate rotation. Rotation must be done in CAM software before generating G-code.
LinuxCNC does not natively support G68. Some third-party add-ons may provide it.

modeNotes.description: Particularly useful for CNC operations where workpiece is not aligned with machine axes.

codes.notes: UNIQUE to RRF among hobby CNC firmwares. Extremely useful for CNC work where the workpiece is not perfectly aligned with machine axes. Cancel rotation with G69. This is a feature typically found only on industrial CNC controllers.

codes.name: Cancel Coordinate Rotation
codes.description: Cancel any coordinate rotation set by G68. Returns to the original coordinate system orientation.

crossReferences.note: Not available in grbl (no coordinate rotation support).

codes.notes: Cancels rotation set by G68.

codes.name: Absolute Distance Mode
codes.description: Set absolute positioning for all axes including extruder (unless M83 is active).

crossReferences.note: Same for XYZ. In RRF, G90 also affects the extruder axis unless M83 relative extrusion mode is active.
Same behavior for linear axes.

codes.notes: In RRF, G90 affects ALL axes including the extruder. Use M82/M83 to independently control extruder positioning mode. Most 3D printing slicers use G90 with M83 (absolute XYZ, relative E).

codes.name: Incremental Distance Mode
codes.description: Set incremental (relative) positioning for all axes including extruder.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior for XYZ.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Affects all axes including extruder unless overridden by M82/M83.

codes.name: Set Position
codes.description: Set the current position to the specified values without moving. Applies an offset to the current coordinate system.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. Both apply a coordinate offset. Note: grbl persists G92 offset across resets; RRF does not.
Same behavior. LinuxCNC persists G92 across resets by default.

codes.notes: For CNC use, prefer G10 L20 to set work coordinates rather than G92. G92 applies an additional offset on top of the active WCS, which can be confusing. G92 offsets are cleared on reset/reboot.

codes.name: Inverse Time Feed Rate Mode
codes.description: Set inverse time feed rate mode. F value specifies the number of moves per minute (1/time). Each move must include an F word.

examples.description: Enable inverse time feed rate mode
Move should complete in 0.5 minutes (30 seconds)

crossReferences.note: grbl does not support inverse time feed rate mode.
Same behavior per NIST standard.

codes.notes: Added in RRF 3.x. Primarily used for non-Cartesian kinematics (e.g., 5-axis) where units-per-minute feed rates are not meaningful.

codes.name: Units Per Minute Feed Rate Mode
codes.description: Set standard feed rate mode (units per minute). This is the default mode.

examples.description: Return to standard feed rate mode (mm/min)

crossReferences.note: Same behavior per NIST standard.

codes.notes: Default feed rate mode. Added in RRF 3.x.

codes.name: Stop
codes.description: Stop the current job. Runs the stop.g macro file if present. Turns off heaters and motors after timeout.

crossReferences.note: grbl M0 = program pause (requires cycle start to resume). RRF M0 = program stop with macro execution.
LinuxCNC M0 = optional program stop (pauses, operator resumes). RRF M0 runs stop.g macro.

codes.notes: Runs stop.g macro which can contain cleanup commands (park, coolant off, etc.). Different from M112 emergency stop.

codes.name: Sleep
codes.description: Put the machine into sleep mode. Runs sleep.g macro. Turns off motors and heaters.

examples.description: Enter sleep mode, run sleep.g macro

crossReferences.note: grbl M1 = optional program stop. RRF M1 = sleep mode.
LinuxCNC M1 = optional stop. RRF M1 = sleep.

codes.notes: Runs sleep.g macro. Different behavior from NIST standard M1 (optional stop).

codes.name: Spindle On (CW) / Laser On
codes.description: In CNC mode (M453), starts the spindle clockwise. In Laser mode (M452), turns on the laser. Behavior depends on the active mode set by M451/M452/M453. Supports multiple spindles via P parameter.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: Spindle speed (RPM) in CNC mode, or laser power (0.0-1.0) in laser mode

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Spindle number (0-based) when multiple spindles configured

examples.description: CNC mode: start spindle at 24000 RPM
Laser mode: enable laser at 50% power

crossReferences.note: WARNING: Laser power control is fundamentally different. grbl uses S on M3 to set laser power (integer 0-$30 max). RRF in laser mode uses S on G1 moves for per-move power control (0.0-1.0 range). M3 S in RRF laser mode enables the laser but per-move S on G1 overrides.
LinuxCNC M3 sets spindle CW. Same concept but RRF's mode-dependent behavior (M451/M452/M453) is unique.

modeNotes.description: Controls spindle direction and speed via configured spindle. Supports multiple spindles with P parameter.
Enables the laser. However, actual cutting power is typically controlled per-move using S on G1. M3 S sets a default/maximum power level.
No spindle/laser functionality in FDM mode.

codes.notes: RRF's multi-mode system (M451/M452/M453) is unique. The P parameter for multiple spindles is also unique to RRF. In laser mode, the preferred method is S on G1 for per-move power control, not M3 S.

codes.name: Delete SD Card File
codes.description: Deletes a file from the SD card. Takes a filename as an unprefixed string parameter.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: Path to file to delete, relative to the G-code directory

crossReferences.note: Same behavior — delete SD card file.
CRITICAL: In grbl, M30 = program end and rewind (NIST standard), NOT file deletion.
CRITICAL: In LinuxCNC, M30 = program end and rewind with optional pallet shuttle (NIST standard), NOT file deletion.
CRITICAL: In FluidNC, M30 = program end and rewind (grbl-compatible), NOT file deletion.

codes.notes: WARNING: M30 means 'delete file' in RepRapFirmware/Smoothieware but 'program end' in CNC firmwares (grbl, grblHAL, FluidNC, LinuxCNC). Sending M30 with a filename to a CNC controller will not delete anything — it will end the program.

codes.name: Spindle On (CCW)
codes.description: Starts spindle counter-clockwise in CNC mode. In laser mode, same as M3.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Spindle number when multiple spindles configured

examples.description: CNC mode: start spindle CCW at 18000 RPM

crossReferences.note: In grbl, M4 = dynamic laser power mode (power scales with speed). RRF has no such M3/M4 distinction — dynamic power is handled differently via S on G1.

modeNotes.description: Counter-clockwise spindle rotation.
Same as M3. No dynamic vs constant power distinction like grbl M3/M4.

codes.notes: In RRF, M3 and M4 differ only in spindle direction. There is no grbl-style M3-constant vs M4-dynamic laser power distinction.

codes.name: Spindle Off / Laser Off
codes.description: Stop the spindle or turn off the laser.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Spindle number to stop (if multiple spindles)

crossReferences.note: Same behavior — stop spindle/laser.

codes.notes: Stops all spindles if no P parameter specified.

codes.name: Tool Change
codes.description: Change to a specified tool. RRF uses a macro-based tool change system with separate macro files for each phase: tfreeN.g (free current tool), tpreN.g (pre-select new tool), tpostN.g (post-select new tool).

parameters.name: T
parameters.description: Tool number to change to (often used as T command rather than M6)

crossReferences.note: grbl ignores M6 by default. grblHAL can handle M6 with tool change macros. RRF has a comprehensive macro-based tool change system.
LinuxCNC M6 performs tool change with manual or automatic tool changer. RRF uses .g macro files for full customization.

modeNotes.description: Tool change macros can include spindle stop, movement to tool change position, probing for tool length, etc.

codes.notes: UNIQUE approach: RRF uses .g macro files for tool changes. The sequence is: tfreeN.g (free old tool) -> tpreN.g (prepare new tool) -> tpostN.g (activate new tool). This provides enormous flexibility for custom tool changers, manual tool changes with probing, etc. Tools are defined with M563.

codes.name: Mist Coolant On
codes.description: Turn on mist coolant.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. grbl uses coolant pins defined in cpu_map.h.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Requires coolant output to be configured in config.g.

codes.name: Flood Coolant On
codes.description: Turn on flood coolant.

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Requires coolant output to be configured in config.g.

codes.name: All Coolant Off
codes.description: Turn off all coolant (both mist and flood).

crossReferences.note: Same behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Turns off both mist (M7) and flood (M8) coolant.

codes.name: Enable Motors
codes.description: Enable (power on) stepper motors.

examples.description: Enable X and Y motors only

codes.notes: Motors are automatically enabled when movement commands are issued.

codes.name: Disable Motors
codes.description: Disable (release) stepper motors. Same as M84. Machine will lose position after this command.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: Idle timeout in seconds (0 = disable immediately)

examples.description: Set motor idle timeout to 30 seconds

codes.notes: WARNING: Disabling motors loses the current position. The machine must be re-homed after motors are disabled.

codes.name: Absolute Extruder Mode
codes.description: Set the extruder to absolute positioning mode, independent of the G90/G91 state.

modeNotes.description: Controls how E values are interpreted in G0/G1 commands.

codes.notes: Only relevant for 3D printing (FDM mode). Independent from G90/G91 axis positioning mode.

codes.name: Relative Extruder Mode
codes.description: Set the extruder to relative positioning mode, independent of the G90/G91 state. Most slicers use this mode.

modeNotes.description: E values on G0/G1 are relative (amount to extrude, not absolute position).

codes.notes: Most 3D printing slicers use G90 (absolute XYZ) with M83 (relative E). This combination is the standard for modern 3D printing.

codes.name: Call Macro
codes.description: Call a macro file from the SD card. Fundamental to RRF's architecture — config.g, homing files, tool change files are all macros. In RRF 3.x, macros support conditional G-code (if/elif/else/while) and expressions.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Macro filename (path relative to /sys or /macros directory)

parameters.name: R
parameters.description: Number: restart the macro from the given line after power failure recovery

examples.description: Run a macro from the macros directory

crossReferences.note: grbl has no macro system. All logic must be in the G-code sender software.
LinuxCNC uses O-word subroutines (O100 call). RRF uses file-based macros with M98/M99.

codes.notes: Fundamental to RRF's architecture. Everything in RRF is macro-driven: config.g, homeall.g, tool change files, bed.g, etc. RRF 3.x added conditional G-code support (if/elif/else/while) and meta-commands, making macros extremely powerful.

codes.name: Return from Macro
codes.description: Return from a macro file called by M98. Execution continues at the line after the M98 call.

crossReferences.note: LinuxCNC uses O-word return. RRF uses M99.

codes.notes: Implicit at end of file — not required if the macro runs to the end. Useful for early return (e.g., in conditional logic).

codes.name: Emergency Stop
codes.description: Immediate emergency stop. Shuts down all motors, heaters, and other outputs immediately. Requires M999 to recover.

examples.description: Emergency stop — all outputs off immediately

crossReferences.note: grbl uses Ctrl-X (0x18) for soft reset. M112 is not standard in grbl.
Same concept — immediate emergency stop.

codes.notes: This is a hard stop. All outputs are immediately disabled. Machine must be reset with M999 and re-homed before further operation.

codes.name: Get Current Position
codes.description: Report the current position of all axes.

crossReferences.note: grbl uses ? for real-time position reporting. M114 is not real-time in RRF — it reports position after buffered moves complete.

codes.notes: Reports position after all buffered moves are complete. For real-time position, use the object model (RRF 3.x).

codes.name: Get Endstop Status
codes.description: Report the current status of all configured endstops.
codes.notes: Useful for debugging homing and endstop wiring issues.

codes.name: Wait for Moves to Finish
codes.description: Wait for all buffered moves to complete before processing the next command.

examples.description: Wait for all pending moves to complete

crossReferences.note: grbl processes commands sequentially with its planner buffer. No explicit M400 equivalent needed — use G4 P0 for synchronization.
LinuxCNC processes commands synchronously in interpreter. M400 is not standard in LinuxCNC.

codes.notes: Important for synchronizing moves with other commands (e.g., wait for positioning before probing, wait before reading sensors).

codes.name: Select FDM Printer Mode
codes.description: Switch the firmware to FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling) 3D printing mode. This is the default mode. Disables spindle/laser control, enables extruder/heater functionality.

crossReferences.note: UNIQUE to RRF: Runtime mode switching. grbl requires recompilation or $32 setting for laser mode only. RRF can switch between FDM/Laser/CNC at runtime.
UNIQUE to RRF: LinuxCNC does not have runtime mode switching. Machine type is determined by HAL configuration.

codes.notes: CRITICAL mode switch command. Part of RRF's unique M451/M452/M453 system. Changes how M3/M4/M5 and S parameter on G1 behave. Default mode on startup unless changed in config.g.

codes.name: Select Laser Mode
codes.description: Switch the firmware to laser cutting/engraving mode. Changes how G1 S parameter works — S on G1 directly controls laser power (0.0-1.0). G0 moves automatically disable laser output.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Logical pin number for laser PWM control

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: Intensity: S parameter represents full power

examples.description: Enable laser mode on pin 2, 0-255 range, 1kHz PWM
Switch to laser mode with current configuration

crossReferences.note: CRITICAL DIFFERENCE: grbl uses $32=1 to enable laser mode, then S on M3/M4 sets power (integer 0-$30 max). RRF uses M452 for laser mode, then S on G1 sets power per-move (0.0-1.0 range). Fundamentally different laser power control paradigm.
LinuxCNC uses M3 S for spindle/laser power control. RRF's M452 mode with S-on-G1 is unique.

modeNotes.description: When active: S on G1 controls laser power (0.0-1.0). G0 moves turn off laser. M3/M5 can enable/disable the laser globally.

codes.notes: CRITICAL: M452 fundamentally changes how the S parameter on G1 works. In laser mode, S on G1 sets laser power (0.0-1.0 range). G0 moves automatically disable the laser. This is the opposite of grbl where laser power is set with M3/M4 S and remains constant until changed. RRF's approach gives per-move power control.

codes.name: Select CNC Mode
codes.description: Switch the firmware to CNC milling mode. Enables spindle control via M3/M4/M5. Spindle speed set with S parameter on M3/M4.

examples.description: Enable CNC mode, spindle 0, max 24000 RPM, 1kHz PWM
Switch to CNC mode with current configuration

crossReferences.note: UNIQUE to RRF: grbl is always in CNC mode (with optional $32 for laser). RRF requires M453 to enable spindle control.
UNIQUE to RRF: LinuxCNC does not need a mode switch for spindle control.

modeNotes.description: When active: M3/M4 S controls spindle speed in RPM. M5 stops spindle. S on G1 has no laser effect.

codes.notes: CRITICAL: Must be set in config.g for CNC machines. Without M453, spindle commands (M3/M4/M5) will not work. Part of RRF's unique runtime mode switching system.

codes.name: Object Cancellation
codes.description: Cancel individual objects during a print. Allows cancelling a single failed part while the rest of the print continues.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: Object index being defined (-1 to indicate non-object moves)

modeNotes.description: Primarily used in FDM printing to cancel individual failed parts.

codes.notes: Available in RRF 3.3+. Slicers like PrusaSlicer and SuperSlicer can automatically add M486 labels to G-code.

codes.name: Save Settings
codes.description: Save current settings to the config-override.g file on the SD card.

crossReferences.note: grbl saves settings automatically to EEPROM with $ commands. RRF saves to config-override.g file.

codes.notes: Settings are saved to config-override.g, which can be loaded at startup by adding M501 at the end of config.g.

codes.name: Load Settings
codes.description: Load settings from config-override.g. Usually placed at the end of config.g to load saved overrides at startup.
codes.notes: Typically the last command in config.g to apply any saved overrides.

codes.name: Report Settings
codes.description: Report current configuration settings.

crossReferences.note: grbl uses $$ to display settings. RRF uses M503.

codes.notes: Outputs the current configuration in G-code format.

codes.name: Set Network State
codes.description: Enable or disable network interface (Ethernet or WiFi).
codes.notes: Part of RRF's built-in network stack. Duet boards have built-in Ethernet or WiFi.

codes.name: Set Netmask
codes.description: Set the network subnet mask.
codes.notes: Only needed for static IP configuration.

codes.name: Set Gateway
codes.description: Set the network gateway address.
codes.notes: Only needed for static IP configuration.

codes.name: Add WiFi Network
codes.description: Store WiFi network credentials for automatic connection.

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: Static IP address (optional, uses DHCP if omitted)

parameters.name: J
parameters.description: Gateway IP (required if using static IP)

parameters.name: K
parameters.description: Subnet mask (required if using static IP)

codes.notes: Credentials are stored persistently. Use M552 S1 to connect after adding credentials.

codes.name: Remove WiFi Network
codes.description: Remove stored WiFi network credentials.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: WiFi SSID to remove. Use S"*" to remove all.

codes.notes: Removes credentials from persistent storage.

codes.name: Configure WiFi Access Point
codes.description: Configure the Duet to act as a WiFi access point.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Password for the access point (min 8 characters)

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: IP address for the access point

codes.notes: Used when the Duet needs to create its own WiFi network (e.g., for initial setup or when no infrastructure network is available).

codes.name: Input Shaping
codes.description: Configure input shaping to reduce ringing/ghosting artifacts caused by mechanical resonances.
codes.notes: Requires tuning to find the correct resonant frequency of the machine. Use an accelerometer for best results. Available shaping algorithms vary by RRF version.

codes.name: Set Kinematics Type
codes.description: Configure the machine kinematics type. RRF supports Cartesian, CoreXY, Delta, SCARA, Polar, rotary delta, Hangprinter, and more.

crossReferences.note: grbl only supports Cartesian kinematics (or CoreXY with compile-time option). RRF supports many kinematics types configurable at runtime.

codes.notes: Must be set in config.g before any movement commands. RRF's runtime-configurable kinematics is a unique feature.

codes.name: Set Motor Current
codes.description: Set the peak current for stepper motors in milliamps. Duet boards have integrated stepper drivers with software-configurable current.

parameters.name: E
parameters.description: Extruder motor current in mA (colon-separated for multiple)

examples.description: Set motor currents with 30% idle current

crossReferences.note: grbl does not control motor current — set via hardware potentiometer or DIP switches on external drivers. RRF has software-configurable motor current.

codes.notes: Duet boards have onboard TMC stepper drivers with software current control. This is a significant advantage over grbl-based boards that require hardware adjustment.

codes.name: Set Motor Current Percentage
codes.description: Set motor current as a percentage of the value set by M906. Useful for reducing current during specific operations.

examples.description: Reduce X and Y motors to 50% of configured current
Restore all motors to full configured current

codes.notes: Useful for sensorless homing (reduce current to make stall detection more sensitive) or reducing holding current during idle periods.

codes.name: Reset Controller
codes.description: Reset the controller board. Used to recover from M112 emergency stop or to reboot the firmware.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: PERASE — reset to factory defaults (use with caution)

crossReferences.note: grbl uses Ctrl-X (0x18) for soft reset. RRF uses M999.

codes.notes: Required after M112 emergency stop. The board re-reads config.g on reset. Use P"PERASE" for factory reset only as a last resort — it erases all configuration.

codes.name: Conditional: if/elif/else
codes.description: Conditional execution of G-code blocks. Evaluates a boolean expression and executes the indented body if true. Supports elif for additional conditions and else for a default branch. The body of each branch must be indented from the keyword. Introduced in RepRapFirmware 3.01.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: Boolean expression using object model values, variables, operators, and functions

examples.description: Home if X or Y not homed
Break if calibration deviation is acceptable

crossReferences.note: Not available in grbl.
grblHAL uses o<name> if [...] syntax from LinuxCNC NGC. RRF uses indentation-based blocks and object model expressions.
LinuxCNC uses o-word if/endif syntax. RRF uses indentation-based syntax with object model access.
FluidNC does not support conditional G-code.

modeNotes.description: In CNC mode, round brackets () are treated as comments. Use curly braces {} for sub-expressions within meta commands.

codes.notes: Available since RRF 3.01. Indentation determines block scope — the body ends at the first line that is not indented from the keyword.

codes.name: Loop: while
codes.description: Repeats an indented block of G-code while a boolean expression is true. The built-in constant 'iterations' provides the zero-based count of completed iterations (0 during first pass, 1 during second, etc.).

examples.code:
while true
  if iterations = 10
    abort "Too many attempts"
  G30 P0 X0 Y140 Z-99999
  if result != 0
    continue
  if move.calibration.initial.deviation <= 0.03
    break

crossReferences.note: Not available in grbl.
grblHAL uses o<name> while [...] syntax. RRF uses indentation-based blocks.
LinuxCNC uses o-word while/endwhile. RRF uses indentation.

codes.notes: WARNING: An infinite loop (while true without break) will hang the machine with no way to exit except a hardware reset. Always include a bounded exit condition. In job files (not macros), use M400 at the end of the loop and before continue statements. Nested loops each have their own 'iterations' counter — save outer counters to variables to access them.

codes.name: Break (Exit Loop)
codes.description: Exits the current while loop immediately. Control transfers to the line after the loop body.

crossReferences.note: grblHAL uses o<name> break. Same concept.
LinuxCNC does not have a break statement for O-word loops.

codes.name: Continue (Next Iteration)
codes.description: Skips the rest of the current loop body, increments the iteration counter, and re-evaluates the while condition.

examples.code:
while true
  G30 P0 X0 Y140 Z-99999
  if result != 0
    continue
examples.description: Skip to next iteration if probe failed

crossReferences.note: grblHAL uses o<name> continue. Same concept.

codes.notes: In job files (not macros), place M400 immediately before continue.

codes.name: Declare Local Variable
codes.description: Creates a new local variable with the given name and initial value. The variable is accessed as var.<name> and is scoped to the remainder of the block in which it is declared. Supported types: bool, int, float, string, arrays (RRF 3.5+).

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Variable name (must start with a letter, followed by letters/digits/underscores)

crossReferences.note: grblHAL uses #<name> = value for named parameters. RRF uses var <name> = value.
LinuxCNC uses #<name> = value for named parameters.

codes.notes: Available since RRF 3.3. Total expression length using the variable must be < 250 characters.

codes.name: Declare Global Variable
codes.description: Creates a new global variable with the given name and initial value. Global variables persist across macros and are accessed as global.<name> from any context. The name must not already be in use.

crossReferences.note: grblHAL global parameters use #<_name> (predefined) or #number (numbered). RRF uses explicit global keyword.

codes.notes: Available since RRF 3.3. Global variables are accessible from all macros and the job file. Typically declared in config.g or a startup macro.

codes.name: Assign Variable
codes.description: Assigns a new value to an existing local (var.) or global (global.) variable. The variable must already exist — use var or global to create it first.

crossReferences.note: grblHAL uses #<name> = value for reassignment. RRF requires the set keyword.

codes.name: Echo (Output)
codes.description: Outputs one or more expressions to the console, converted to strings with spaces between each pair. Can also redirect output to files using >, >>, or >>> operators.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: One or more comma-separated expressions to output

examples.description: Print homed status of X and Y
Write G-code to a new file

crossReferences.note: grblHAL uses (print, ...) comments for output. RRF uses echo keyword with full expression support.
LinuxCNC uses (print, ...) comment syntax.

codes.notes: File output with > (create), >> (append line), >>> (append no newline, RRF 3.5+). Default folder is /sys. echo only outputs to the channel it was received on — use M118 to send to all channels.

codes.name: Abort (Stop with Message)
codes.description: Terminates all nested macros and the current print file. The optional expression is converted to a string and included in the error message presented to the user and written to the log.

examples.code:
if sensors.probes[0].value[0] > 1000
  abort "Probe error: value out of range"

crossReferences.note: grblHAL uses o<name> alarm [code] or (abort, message) for similar functionality.
LinuxCNC uses M2 for program end or custom error handling through HAL.

codes.notes: Unlike M112 (emergency stop), abort does not require M999 to recover. It simply terminates the current execution context.

codes.name: Expressions and Object Model
codes.description: RepRapFirmware supports inline expressions using {expression} syntax within any G-code command. Expressions can access the full object model (machine state, sensor values, positions, etc.), use arithmetic/comparison/logical operators, built-in math functions, and the ternary operator.

crossReferences.note: grblHAL uses [...] for expressions with #-parameters. RRF uses {...} with object model access.
LinuxCNC uses [...] for expressions. RRF's object model provides far more accessible machine state.

modeNotes.description: In CNC mode, round brackets () are treated as comments. Use them for sub-expressions only within {} curly braces.

codes.notes: Key object model paths: move.axes[n].machinePosition, move.axes[n].homed, move.axes[n].max/min, heat.heaters[n].current/active, state.currentTool, sensors.probes[n].value[0], move.calibration.initial.deviation. Operators: +, -, *, /, = (or ==), !=, <, <=, >, >=, & (and), | (or), ^ (string concat), ! (not), # (length), ? : (ternary). Functions: abs, acos, asin, atan, atan2, ceil, cos, degrees, exists, exp, fileexists, floor, isnan, log, max, min, mod, pow, radians, random, round, sin, sqrt, tan, vector.
